chunks.page_summary: This page provides instructions and technical details on how to set the density input for a Rheonics SRV inline viscometer to obtain accurate dynamic and kinematic viscosity outputs, outlining both constant and temperature-dependent density models.
chunks.extracted_text:
Phone: +1 713 234 0863

Set density input to SRV inline viscometer for dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity outputs

Modified on: Sun, 15 Feb, 2026 at 12:52 AM

Rheonics SRV measures viscous damping, which is directly proportional to any change in the process fluid viscosity and density. The sensor then gives the user an output that is a product of dynamic viscosity and density. The fluid dynamic viscosity is obtained by dividing the previous product by the density. By default, density is set to 1.0 g/cc (1000 kg/m3).

If fluid density is known and different to 1.0 g/cc, the sensor supports input of a constant or temperature dependent density so dynamic and kinematic viscosity values can be estimated and output on the viscosity channel. With density input, the dynamic and kinematic viscosity can be directly output by the sensor over digital and analog channels without a need for any PC.

The steps below show how the user can set the density on the sensor using the RCP software and Modbus RTU/TCP to get dynamic or kinematic viscosity directly from the sensor electronics as well as on the RCP software.

Enter fluid density to get dynamic and kinematic viscosity from SRV

1. Introduction to density input for SRV

The Density SRV output can be described as a polynomial of the form:

D_out = D_05 . T^5 + D_04 . T^4 + D_03 . T^3 + D_02 . T^2 + D_01 . T + D_00

With this model we can describe a constant density or a temperature dependent density.

a. Constant density input
To describe a constant density, the coefficient D_00 should be set equal to the constant density value, and all the other coefficients should be set to zero.

b. Temperature dependent density input
To describe a temperature dependent density, assign values to the coefficients, that reflects the density behavior of your fluid with temperature. If you have a fluid reference table (for example Cannon fluids, Nippon grease), you can fit the density to a polynomial model to get the coefficients.

The equation above expects temperature in °C. If you have data in °F or °K, then convert temperature to corresponding °C before estimating the coefficients.
key_facts: Rheonics SRV measures viscous damping.
Viscous damping is directly proportional to any change in the process fluid viscosity and density.
The sensor's output is a product of dynamic viscosity and density.
Fluid dynamic viscosity is obtained by dividing the sensor's product output by the density.
Kinematic viscosity is obtained by dividing the product by the density.
By default, the density for calculations is set to 1.0 g/cc (1000 kg/m3).
The sensor supports input of a constant or temperature-dependent density if the actual fluid density is known and differs from 1.0 g/cc.
With density input, dynamic and kinematic viscosity values can be estimated and output on the viscosity channel.
Density input allows dynamic and kinematic viscosity to be directly output by the sensor over digital and analog channels without requiring a PC.
Users can set the density on the sensor using Rheonics RCP software and Modbus RTU/TCP.
The Density SRV output can be described by a 5th-order polynomial: D_out = D_05 . T^5 + D_04 . T^4 + D_03 . T^3 + D_02 . T^2 + D_01 . T + D_00.
For a constant density input, the coefficient D_00 should be set to the constant density value, and all other coefficients (D_01 to D_05) should be set to zero.

For a temperature-dependent density input, coefficients should be assigned to reflect the fluid's density behavior with temperature, potentially by fitting data from a fluid reference table (e.g., Cannon fluids, Nippon grease) to the polynomial model.
warnings_notes: The polynomial equation for density input expects temperature in °C. If temperature data is available in °F or °K, it must be converted to °C before estimating the coefficients.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides step-by-step instructions for setting the density parameter for an SRV sensor using the Rheonics Control Panel (RCP) software, offering options for constant or temperature-dependent density configurations.
chunks.extracted_text:
Once you have the coefficients (Do0, Do1, ...), you can use any of the methods described below to upload these into the sensor electronics (SME).

2. Setting density for SRV sensor

Using RCP

a. From RCP Navigate to the "Service" tab

[Screenshot: Rheonics Control Panel 5.3.0.0 Interface]
  System Info:
    Sensor S/N: B05-0163
    Electronics S/N: 19101382
  Software Info:
    Interface FW Version: 2.10/5-df9e0bf
    Sensor FW Version: SWV200f
  Update Values:
    Refresh button

b. Look for the "Density (SRV Only)" section: (this is only active when you have a SRV sensor connected to RCP)

[Screenshot: Density (SRV Only) section, Constant option selected]
  Set Density:
    Constant: 1 g/cc (radio button selected)
    Temp. Dependent Density Curve (T,°C) (radio button unselected)
  Density, g/cc:
    0.000 xT^2 + 0.000 xT + 1.000
  Write button

c. Select between Constant Density or Temperature Dependent Density (Polynomial)
d. For constant density, edit the value and click "Write".

[Screenshot: Density (SRV Only) section, Constant value '1' and 'Write' button highlighted]
  Set Density:
    Constant: 1 g/cc (radio button selected)
    Temp. Dependent Density Curve (T,°C) (radio button unselected)
  Density, g/cc:
    0.000 xT^2 + 0.000 xT + 1.000
  Write button
key_facts: This document describes methods for uploading density coefficients (Do0, Do1, ...) into the sensor electronics (SME).
The Rheonics Control Panel (RCP) software is used to set the density for the SRV sensor.
Users must navigate to the 'Service' tab in the RCP software to access density settings.
The 'Density (SRV Only)' section in RCP is only active when an SRV sensor is connected.
Density can be set as a 'Constant' value or as a 'Temperature Dependent Density Curve (Polynomial)'.
To apply a constant density value, the user must edit the value and then click the 'Write' button.
warnings_notes: The 'Density (SRV Only)' section is only active when a SRV sensor is connected to RCP.

chunks.page_summary: This page details how to configure density settings for SRV sensors, including temperature-dependent polynomial coefficients, and provides Modbus RTU/TCP communication instructions and register mapping for density input.
chunks.extracted_text:
e. For temperature dependent. Input the coefficients and click "Write". Ensure Temperature (T) was in °C when estimating the polynomial coefficients.

Density (SRV Only)
Set Density
Constant
1 g/cc
Temp. Dependent Density Curve (T, °C)
?
Write
Density, g/cc
0.001 xT^2 + 0.003 xT + 1.000

Over Modbus RTU/TCP
Over Modbus (either RTU or TCP) there are a set of registers associated with the density input for the SRV sensors.
There are two holding registers associated with each of the Do coefficients of the polynomial.
The two holding registers of each coefficient should form a float 32 data type in Big Endian format.
The registers are password protected. This is, you should input the user password in the holding register 0 to be able to write to the coefficients.
You can use the software of your preference to write to the holding register. The write function is 16 to write to multiple registers, and 03 to read the holding registers.

| Register | Holding Register Address | Data Type |
|---|---|---|
| Password | Address = 0; User Password = 0x5312 | Uint16 |
| Do0 | 1212 | Float32 |
| Do1 | 1214 | Float32 |
| Do2 | 1216 | Float32 |
| Do3 | 1218 | Float32 |
| Do4 | 1220 | Float32 |
| Do5 | 1222 | Float32 |

An example using ModbusPoll software to get constant density:
a. Write the password into the holding register 0.
key_facts: Temperature-dependent density configuration involves inputting polynomial coefficients and clicking 'Write'.
The temperature (T) used for estimating polynomial coefficients must be in °C.
Modbus RTU or TCP is used for density input with SRV sensors.
Each 'Do' coefficient of the polynomial is represented by two holding registers.
These two holding registers for each coefficient form a Float32 data type in Big Endian format.
The registers are password protected, requiring the user password to be entered into holding register 0 to enable writing.
The Modbus write function is 16 for writing to multiple registers.
The Modbus read function is 03 for reading holding registers.
ModbusPoll software can be used, for example, to write the password into holding register 0 to set constant density.
warnings_notes: Ensure Temperature (T) was in °C when estimating the polynomial coefficients.
The registers are password protected; the user password must be input in holding register 0 to write to coefficients.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides instructions and examples for configuring registers and coefficients using a software interface, likely for a device's operational parameters.
chunks.extracted_text:
Write Single Register
Slave ID: 255
Address: 0
Value (HEX): 5312
Result: N/A
[Checkbox] Close dialog on "Response ok" (checked)
Use Function
[Radio button] 06: Write single register (selected)
[Radio button] 16: Write multiple registers
[Button] Send
[Button] Cancel

b. Write constant value to coefficients Do0, and set all other coefficients to 0.

poll-coeff2
Tx = 106: Err = 0: ID = 255: F = 03: SR = 10

Table:
| Address | Alias | Value (01100) |
|---------|-------|---------------|
| 1212    | Do0   | 1             |
| 1213    | --    | --            |
| 1214    | Do1   | 0             |
| 1215    | --    | --            |
| 1216    | Do2   | 0             |
| 1217    | --    | --            |
| 1218    | Do3   | 0             |
| 1219    | --    | --            |
| 1220    | Do4   | 0             |
| 1221    | --    | --            |
| 1222    | Do5   | 0             |
| 1223    | --    | --            |

For temperature dependence, Write to the set of coefficients to get the expected behavior, according to the fluid and application.
key_facts: The 'Write Single Register' interface allows configuration of Slave ID (255), Address (0), and a hexadecimal Value (5312).
The option '06: Write single register' is selected for writing to a single register.
Instruction 'b' specifies writing a constant value to coefficient Do0 and setting all other coefficients to 0.
The 'poll-coeff2' window displays communication parameters: Tx = 106, Err = 0, ID = 255, F = 03, SR = 10.
Coefficients Do0 through Do5 are listed with their respective addresses and values, showing Do0 as '1' (at address 1212) and Do1-Do5 as '0' (at addresses 1214, 1216, 1218, 1220, 1222).
For temperature dependence, adjusting the set of coefficients is necessary to achieve the desired behavior, which varies based on the fluid and application.

Using configuration file from Rheonics Support

Rheonics support team can help you configure the Density input to SRV for your application. For that, you will get a configuration file that will be valid ONLY for the specific sensor it was created for.

a. Open RCP
b. Go to "Settings" tab and click on "Expert Mode"

key_facts: Rheonics support team provides configuration files for the Density input to SRV.
Configuration files are sensor-specific and valid ONLY for the sensor they were created for.
The Rheonics Control Panel (RCP) is used for sensor configuration.
Accessing 'Expert Mode' in the RCP's 'Settings' tab requires a password.
The password for 'Expert Mode' can be found in the RCP manual.
The RCP allows configuration of sensor type, communication settings (USB, Ethernet, Bluetooth), and units/resolution for viscosity, density, temperature, and pressure.
Rheonics GmbH is listed as a client.
warnings_notes: A configuration file will be valid ONLY for the specific sensor it was created for.
Password for Expert mode can be found in the RCP manual.

chunks.page_summary: This page describes how to access and manage advanced sensor configuration settings within the Rheonics Control Panel software, including warnings, operational steps, and UI elements for importing and exporting sensor configuration files.
chunks.extracted_text:
Warning
Advanced functionality enabled in RCP allows users to make changes to the sensor that can change the measurement outputs.
If you are trained and experienced to perform these changes, switch on the expert mode. Otherwise, contact your local support or Rheonics global support at support.rheonics.com to get help implementing the advanced functionality needed.
Password can be found in the RCP manual
Access Password: ********
Cancel Yes
d. Now a new set of buttons will appear or be enabled in the software:
Rheonics Control Panel 5.3.0.0
Measurement Events Settings Servi
Operator
Name / ID: Rheonics
Client: Rheonics GmbH
Sensor Configuration
Sensor Type: SRV
Import Export
Disable Expert Mode
Factory Reset Power Reset
e. Click "Import" button to load the file provided by rheonics support team into the sensor.
f. "Import" button should turn GREEN for a moment if the file was properly loaded. Otherwise it will turn red. If that's the case please, click "Refresh" button and re-attempt loading the file.
g. The "Export" button will automatically triggered. This will generate a configuration file that should be sent back to rheonics team for verification.
h. Click "Refresh" to populate and review the new settings in software.
Steps are described in detail in the support article Updating sensor configuration
(https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000390438)
key_facts: Advanced functionality in the Rheonics Control Panel (RCP) allows users to alter sensor measurement outputs.
Accessing advanced functionality requires users to be trained and experienced, necessitating a switch to expert mode.
If users are not trained or experienced, they should contact their local Rheonics support or global support at support.rheonics.com for assistance with advanced functionality.
The password for advanced access can be found in the RCP manual.
Clicking the "Import" button loads a configuration file provided by the Rheonics support team into the sensor.
A successful file import is indicated by the "Import" button briefly turning GREEN; failure results in the button turning RED.
If a file fails to load, the user should click "Refresh" and re-attempt the loading process.
The "Export" button automatically generates a configuration file that needs to be sent to the Rheonics team for verification.
After making configuration changes, clicking "Refresh" is necessary to populate and review the new settings in the software.
Detailed steps for updating sensor configuration are available in a Rheonics support article.
warnings_notes: Advanced functionality enabled in RCP allows users to make changes to the sensor that can change the measurement outputs.

If you are trained and experienced to perform these changes, switch on the expert mode. Otherwise, contact your local support or Rheonics global support at support.rheonics.com to get help implementing the advanced functionality needed.
Password can be found in the RCP manual.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides instructions on how to configure and view dynamic viscosity output from an SRV sensor using the Rheonics Control Panel software and how to access dynamic viscosity data via Modbus.
chunks.extracted_text:
3. Dynamic viscosity output from SRV

Using RCP
Since SRV measures a product of Density and Dynamic Viscosity of the fluid, to get the correct Dynamic Viscosity measurement from the SRV, the density should be input following any of the steps described above.
To display and log the viscosity, in RCP Software, go to settings page, and in "Graph Settings" section select "Viscosity" from the dropdown list.
This will display the Dynamic Viscosity value in the Measurements tab and also will be saved when logging the records. (Assuming that the actual density has been input as described in the points above).

Rheonics Control Panel 5.3.0.0
  Tabs: Measurement, Events, Settings, Service, Diagnostics, Communication, Temp. Control, Logger, Help
  Operator section:
    Name / ID: Rheonics
    Client: Rheonics GmbH
  Communication section:
    Radio buttons: USB (selected), Ethernet, Bluetooth
    ID: 70:b3:d5:d2:00:f9 (dropdown)
    Button: Autodetect
    IP: 10.100.0.16
    Port: 10001
    Button: Apply
    Button: Device Manager
    Status: (Red indicator)
  Sensor Configuration section:
    Sensor Type: - no sensor type - (dropdown)
    Button: Import
    Button: Export
    Button: Expert Mode
  Units section:
    Viscosity: mPa.s (selected), cP, cSt
    Density: g/cc (selected), kg/m³, lb/gal, lb/ft³
    Temperature: °C (selected), °K, °F
    Pressure: bar (selected), kPa, MPa, psi, torr, atm
    Resolution column:
      Viscosity: 0.0000 (dropdown)
      Density: 0.0000 (dropdown)
      Temperature: 0.00 (dropdown)
      Pressure: 0.00 (dropdown)
  Pressure Gauge section:
    Communication: (dropdown)
    Device ID: No Sensor
  Graph Settings section:
    Viscosity: (dropdown with Viscosity selected) auto (selected) / fix
      Min: 1 mPa.s
      Max: 40 mPa.s
    Density: (dropdown) auto (selected) / fix
      Min: 0 g/cc
      Max: 1.5 g/cc
    Temperature Scale: auto (selected) / fix
      Min: 10 °C
      Max: 60 °C
    Pressure Scale: auto (selected) / fix
      Min: 1 bar
      Max: 10 bar
    Time Scale: full (selected) / fix
      Range: 5 min (dropdown)
    Graph Interval: realtime (selected) / same as logging
    Checkbox: Plot df (damping) and Fres (resonant frequency) instead of Viscosity & Density (unchecked)
  Log Settings section:
    Logging Interval: 10 sec (dropdown)
    Measurement Frequency (Every): 1 sec (dropdown)
    Checkbox: Log and display data with error code (unchecked)
    Checkbox: Log debug information: 30 sec (checked, dropdown)

Over Modbus
We can access Dynamic Viscosity over Modbus, these are:
a. Median Dynamic Viscosity (5 points by default. Can be modified with filters) - Parameter 1
b. Raw Dynamic Viscosity - Parameter 6
c. Median and Last Good Dynamic Viscosity - Parameter 13
Last Good holds onto the last valid value of the parameter till a new valid value is generated.
Each parameter is assigned two input registers to build a float 32 data type format.
You can access the registers according to the table below.
key_facts: The SRV measures the product of Density and Dynamic Viscosity of the fluid.
Correct Dynamic Viscosity measurement from the SRV requires the fluid's density to be manually input.
The Rheonics Control Panel (RCP) Software displays and logs Dynamic Viscosity values in the Measurements tab.

Dynamic Viscosity can be accessed over Modbus via three parameters: Median Dynamic Viscosity (Parameter 1), Raw Dynamic Viscosity (Parameter 6), and Median and Last Good Dynamic Viscosity (Parameter 13).
The 'Last Good' Modbus parameter retains the last valid value until a new valid value is generated.
Each Modbus parameter is assigned two input registers to form a float 32 data type.
diagram_labels: Plot df (damping) and Fres (resonant frequency) instead of Viscosity & Density
Log and display data with error code
warnings_notes: (Assuming that the actual density has been input as described in the points above).
Last Good holds onto the last valid value of the parameter till a new valid value is generated.

key_facts: Rheonics provides various dynamic viscosity parameters (Median, Raw, Median and Last Good) accessible via specific Modbus input registers (40-41, 80-81, 136-137), all as Float 32 data type.
Kinematic viscosity output from SRV can be displayed and logged using the Rheonics Control Panel (RCP) software by selecting "Kin. Visc." in the Graph Settings section of the settings page.

The Rheonics Control Panel software (version 5.3.0.0) allows configuration of communication (USB, Ethernet, Bluetooth), sensor type, units (viscosity, density, temperature, pressure), resolution, graph settings, and logging parameters.
The default units shown in the RCP screenshot are cSt for viscosity, g/cc for density, °C for temperature, and bar for pressure.
Kinematic Viscosity can also be accessed directly via Modbus by reading input registers 64-65, which corresponds to Parameter 4 and is of Float 32 data type.
Further details on Modbus protocol and registers are available on the Rheonics Helpdesk at the provided URL.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides step-by-step instructions and an example for manually calculating dynamic and kinematic viscosity from Rheonics SRV sensor outputs, particularly when the sensor's default density setting of 1 g/cc is used.
chunks.extracted_text:
For more details about Modbus protocol, and registers, go to: Helpdesk : Rheonics - Modbus Input Registers
https://rheonics.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/SME/pages/33783828/Parameters+List+and+Status

5. Manually estimating dynamic and kinematic viscosity from SRV outputs

SRV measures the product of Dynamic Viscosity and Density, this is: SRV Viscosity output = Fluid Dynamic Viscosity * Density.

By default SRV Density is set to 1 g/cc. However, as described in steps above, you can change the SRV density. If your measurements are made with the default density, and you want to obtain the kinematic viscosity for those measurements, follow the instructions below.

For a reference fluid in which, the Kinematic Viscosity and Dynamic Viscosity at a certain temperature is known from the fluid reference table, (for example Nippon Grease); we can calculate the Density of the fluid at that temperature.

Density = (Dynamic Viscosity / Kinematic Viscosity) = μ / ν

to calculate the Fluid Dynamic Viscosity from the SRV measurement, we can estimate using:

Fluid Dynamic Viscosity = (SRV Viscosity Output / Density)

and, to calculate the Kinematic Viscosity from the SRV measurement, we can make:

Fluid Kinematic Viscosity = (SRV Viscosity Output / Density^2)

For example:
a. At 26.5 °C, SRV measures a Viscosity value of 21026.7 cP and density was set as the default value of 1 g/cc in the sensor.

b. According to the Nippon Grease reference table:
| Temp (°C) | Kinematic Viscosity (mm²/s) | Dynamic Viscosity (cP) |
|---|---|---|
| 21.4 | 43960 | 38920 |
| 21.5 | 43520 | 38530 |
| 21.6 | 43090 | 38140 |
| 21.7 | 42660 | 37760 |
| 21.8 | 42230 | 37380 |
| 21.9 | 41810 | 37000 |
| 26.4 | 26980 | 23820 |
| 26.5 | 26730 | 23590 |
| 26.6 | 26480 | 23370 |
| 26.7 | 26230 | 23150 |
| 26.8 | 25980 | 22930 |
| 26.9 | 25740 | 22710 |
| 31.4 | 17010 | 14970 |
| 31.5 | 16860 | 14840 |
| 31.6 | 16710 | 14710 |
| 31.7 | 16560 | 14570 |
| 31.8 | 16420 | 14440 |
| 31.9 | 16270 | 14310 |
| 36.4 | 10980 | 9637 |
| 36.5 | 10890 | 9555 |
| 36.6 | 10800 | 9473 |
| 36.7 | 10710 | 9392 |
| 36.8 | 10620 | 9312 |
| 36.9 | 10530 | 9232 |
at 26.5 °C the kinematic Viscosity value is 26730 mm2/s and dynamic Viscosity is 23590 cP

c. If we calculate the reference Density at that temperature, it is 23590/26730 = 0.8825 g/cc

d. Then we can calculate the SRV Kinematic Viscosity by using the Kin. Visc. equation above:
Kin.Visc = 21026.7 / 0.8825^2 = 26998 mm²/s

e. According to the table, the expected kinematic viscosity at that temperature, is 26730 mm2/s, that leads to a percentage error of 0.992%. Which is within the rheonics sensors accuracy.
key_facts: SRV sensors measure a product defined as 'Fluid Dynamic Viscosity * Density'.
The default density setting for SRV sensors is 1 g/cc.
Formulas are provided to calculate Fluid Dynamic Viscosity and Fluid Kinematic Viscosity from SRV sensor output and a known fluid density.
For Nippon Grease at 26.5 °C, the calculated SRV Kinematic Viscosity from sensor output is 26998 mm²/s, which is compared to an expected value of 26730 mm²/s from a reference table.
The resulting percentage error of 0.992% in the example calculation is stated to be within the accuracy specifications of Rheonics sensors.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides a note on how the SRV sensor outputs viscosity values based on fluid density input and lists several references and download links for Rheonics products and software.
chunks.extracted_text:
NOTE: if the fluid density at that temperature (0.8825 g/cc) is input, as described in the first section of the document, then SRV will directly output the Dynamic Viscosity value (as well as kinematic viscosity value when selected in either RCP, on the TRD display or over digital channels (Modbus RTU/TCP, Serial over USB, Serial over Ethernet, EthernetIP, etc).

References/Further information

SRV Whitepaper (https://rheonics.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/10/rheonics-vibration-sensor-whitepaper.pdf)

SRV Product Information (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-viscometer-srv/)

RCP - Manual (https://support.rheonics.com/support/solutions/folders/81000211411) RCP manual - Rheonics Control Panel, software to acquire data from rheonics sensors as well as to configure the sensor

Rheonics SensorView app: download for Android (https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.rheonics.rheonicsBLE&hl=es&gl=US) iOS (https://apps.apple.com/us/app/rheonics-sensorview/id1557966204)
key_facts: If the fluid density of 0.8825 g/cc is input, the SRV sensor will directly output Dynamic Viscosity.
The SRV sensor can also output kinematic viscosity if selected in RCP, on the TRD display, or over digital channels.
Digital channels supported include Modbus RTU/TCP, Serial over USB, Serial over Ethernet, and EthernetIP.
The Rheonics Control Panel (RCP) is software used to acquire data and configure Rheonics sensors.
The Rheonics SensorView app is available for download on both Android and iOS devices.
warnings_notes: NOTE: if the fluid density at that temperature (0.8825 g/cc) is input, as described in the first section of the document, then SRV will directly output the Dynamic Viscosity value (as well as kinematic viscosity value when selected in either RCP, on the TRD display or over digital channels (Modbus RTU/TCP, Serial over USB, Serial over Ethernet, EthernetIP, etc).
